questions.scenario:
A module declares:

```hcl
variable "database_password" {
  type      = string
  sensitive = true
}
```

A reviewer says this means the password can no longer appear in Terraform state. What is the best response?

options.text: Sensitive hides normal CLI display, but the real value may still be present in state and must be protected.
Sensitive only works for outputs, not variables.
Sensitive prevents providers from receiving the value, so the database password will not be configured.
Sensitive encrypts the value inside state, so backend access does not matter.

questions.explanation: Terraform sensitivity is mainly a display boundary. It helps keep terminal output and routine logs cleaner, but state and saved plans can still contain the values Terraform needs to operate. See [Terraform sensitive data guidance](https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/language/manage-sensitive-data).

questions.scenario: The orders module receives a database password from the deployment secret store. A proposed output republishes it with `sensitive = true` for a possible future migration script, but no current consumer requires the value and the team wants to minimize secret paths. What is the best review decision?

options.text: Rename it to `secret_password` and keep the output.
Approve it because sensitive outputs are not stored anywhere after apply.
Remove `sensitive = true` so reviewers can inspect the exact password.
Avoid exposing the password unless a real consumer needs it; sensitive redaction does not make the output harmless.

questions.explanation: Outputs are a module contract. A sensitive output still increases the number of places that value travels, and it can still be present in state. Expose it only when there is a deliberate consumer.

questions.scenario: The orders team relies on plan review to confirm whether a change targets `dev`, `staging`, or `prod`. A pull request marks the `environment` input sensitive to make plan output quieter, even though the value is not confidential. Which design preserves useful targeting evidence while enforcing valid choices?

options.text: Move the environment into a password manager because it appears in tags.
Keep environment visible and validate allowed values, because hiding it removes useful operational context.
Hardcode production in the module so no variable appears in the plan.
Mark every variable sensitive so all plans are quieter.

questions.explanation: Sensitivity is for values that should not be exposed. Environment names are usually review context, not secrets. Validation is a better control for allowed environment choices.

questions.scenario: The orders root receives a database password from CI, stores state remotely, and exposes selected module outputs to deployment jobs. Which two controls directly reduce unnecessary secret exposure?

options.text: Mark ordinary environment and owner tags sensitive so production targeting is hidden from routine plan review.
Restrict backend access because state can retain sensitive input and provider-returned values.
Publish the password as a sensitive output so deployment jobs can choose later whether they need it.
Supply the password through an approved secret channel rather than committing it in a `.tfvars` file.

questions.explanation: An approved secret channel keeps cleartext out of Git, and restricted backend access protects values Terraform may retain. A speculative sensitive output expands the value's interface and should exist only for a real consumer. Hiding ordinary targeting tags removes useful review evidence without protecting a secret.

questions.scenario: A migration job needs temporary database access, and a runbook proposes `terraform output -raw database_password` in CI because the output is marked sensitive. CI logs are retained for 30 days and readable by the engineering group. What should the security reviewer require?

options.text: Approve only if the value also appears in a tfvars file.
Do not print it in CI; raw output can reveal a sensitive output value to logs or callers.
Use a local value instead, because locals are always encrypted in state.
Approve because the raw flag preserves redaction for sensitive outputs.

questions.explanation: Sensitive output redaction helps normal display, but raw or JSON output can expose the value to the caller. CI logs and artifacts need the same secret-handling discipline as the backend.

questions.scenario: A CI runner has `TF_VAR_environment=staging` in its environment, but the plan command is `terraform plan -var-file=prod.tfvars` and that file sets `environment = "prod"`. Which value should the resource plan use?

options.text: `staging`, because environment variables always override every command-line value source.
No value, because Terraform rejects any duplicate input source.
Both values, because Terraform converts conflicting strings into a list.
`prod`, because the explicitly named variable file has higher precedence than `TF_VAR_environment`.

questions.explanation: Terraform resolves variable sources by precedence, and a named `-var-file` value overrides a `TF_VAR_` environment value for the same input. Terraform does not combine conflicting strings or reject duplicate sources when precedence resolves them, and `TF_VAR_` does not outrank an explicitly named variable file. The plan should still make the final production name and tags visible before apply. See [Terraform variable assignment precedence](https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/language/parameterize#assign-values-to-input-variables).

questions.scenario: The production plan uses `retention_days = 0` and proposes immediate expiration for export objects. The variable has a number type but no validation. What is the most useful contract improvement?

options.text: Add a string output that prints the zero value after apply.
Add a validation rule that rejects values outside the approved retention range before provider calls.
Move the value into a local so callers can no longer see which retention period was selected.
Mark the retention value sensitive so the risky value disappears from routine plan output.

questions.explanation: A numeric type checks shape, while validation can enforce the business-safe range before Terraform asks the provider to change lifecycle behavior. Sensitivity would only hide useful review context, a local would relocate the value without validating it, and an output would report the unsafe value after the decision. See [Terraform variable validation](https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/language/values/variables#custom-validation-rules).
